text: The robotic surgical view shows the gallbladder with chronic adhesions and inflammation, loosely attached to the duodenum. The left lobe of the liver is visible and obstructing the surgical field, which the surgeon nudges aside using the elbow of the robotic arm.

The surgeon observes significant inflammation around the gallbladder, with a stone visibly lodged in its neck. The cystic artery is illuminated using ICG dye, appearing relatively short, as the surgeon prepares for straightforward arterial dissection.

The surgeon identifies the cystic duct, using the robotic arm to nudge the left lobe of the liver out of the way for better access. Despite the ICG not being helpful, the surgeon clears surrounding tissue to achieve the critical view of safety, with a few small crossing vessels visible near the duct.

The surgeon carefully works on the cystic plate, encountering smaller vessels and visible pericholecystic fluid. The chronic inflammation makes it difficult to distinguish between the gallbladder and the cystic portion of the hepatic plate, requiring slow and meticulous dissection.
